Journal.Title: Frontiers in sports and active living

Article.ArticleTitle: Determinants of pre-adolescent girls' sport performance in three-year swimming training.

Abstract.AbstractText: The aim of the study was to identify which anthropometric, physiological, and respiratory parameters are the most important determinants of 400 m front crawl swimming performance among prepubescent, non-elite female swimmers.

The study group consisted of 14 swimmers (mean biological age at baseline: 10.52 ± 0.37 years; body mass: 34.99 ± 2.77 kg; height: 146.00 ± 3.05 cm). The study was conducted over three years. The swimmers followed endurance training recommended by the British Swimming Federation. Every six months, the following parameters were measured: percentage of body fat, anthropometric variables, aerobic and anaerobic capacity, respiratory parameters [vital capacity [VC], forced expiratory volume in 1 s [FEV1], forced vital capacity [FVC]], and a 400 m front crawl swimming test.

After accounting for multicollinearity, the most influential factors determining 400 m front crawl performance were foot dimensions, VC and % body fat. Other somatic and physiological predictors had a less of an impact on swimming performance.
The 400 m front crawl performance among prepubescent girls is not determined by aerobic capacity but rather by foot length and respiratory indicators.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: © 2025 Kuberski, Musial, Krużołek, Błażkiewicz, Wąsik and Konarski.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: The authors declare that the research was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial relationships that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest.
